title: Ownership Concentration and Corporate Performance on the Budapest Stock Exchange: do too many cooks spoil the goulash?
abstract: We examine the impact of ownership concentration on firm performance using panel data for firms listed on the Budapest Stock Exchange, where ownership tends to be highly concentrated and frequently involves multiple blocks. Fixed-effects estimates imply that the size of the largest block increases profitability and efficiency strongly and monotonically, but the effects of total blockholdings are much smaller and statistically insignificant. Controlling for the size of the largest block, point estimates of the marginal effects of additional blocks are negative. The results suggest that the marginal costs of concentration may outweigh the benefits when the increased concentration involves “too many cooks”.
summary_en: On the Budapest Stock Exchange most firms have multiple large blockholders — not a single controlling owner. Is that good for performance? This paper uses firm-level panel data and firm fixed effects to show that the size of the single largest blockholder strongly raises profitability and efficiency. But adding more blockholders on top of the largest one has negligible, even slightly negative, marginal effects. "Too many cooks spoil the goulash": concentration of control matters, but fragmentation among several blockholders does not help and may hurt coordination.
data_used: Panel of firms listed on the Budapest Stock Exchange, 1996–2001; ownership and financial data from the exchange and BIMBO corporate governance database; ~50 firms annually.
policy_relevance: For Hungarian corporate-governance policy (MNB supervisory role, ownership-transparency rules): the evidence supports rules that protect block-owner rights (since large blocks raise performance), while not idealising multiple-blockholder arrangements. Relevant for listed-firm governance codes and for the design of state-owned-enterprise partial privatisations: if the state retains a minority block alongside other blocks, governance gains may be limited. Setting is a small stock exchange in the late 1990s; applicability to today's much larger Hungarian SOE sector should be cross-checked.
